author: @linuxcaffe
text:
Very exciting Robert! Looks like a great space and DIY graphene is a great focus!

As a filmmaker, I see how important the videos are going to be, and I wanted to suggest a couple of things:

1. get a wireless, clip-on microphone, one part on your belt and on your shirt, the other part plugged into the camera. It will give you great sound no matter where you are standing in the room, facing the camera or not. You can use the same mic with a speaker system, when addressing a larger group.

2. Set the camera exposure manually and turn off auto-exposure. It is not your friend in that environment,

Keep up the great new start, looking forward to seeing your progress.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: thanks mate - good advice and i will take it on board for future vids

author: @das250250
text:
Hi Robert, 
what you will need to fix is the audio , so much reverb  in the new place makes it hard to hear . Sometimes I listen to the video while doing other things and if the audio isn't crisp it just fades . Even watching video can be straining to the ears . 
Suggest you get a wireless lapel mic and input into audio of video machine so it can be placed near collar or shirt . Alternatively  you may  wish to close up the whiteboard area
 with acoustic absorbing material  , books , wood , dividing walls ... to break up the Hall effect reverb .
Love your new place and I would be the first to visit if I was in England , unfortunately here on other side of planet. Upside down I believe..lol

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I have a radio mike but i still am unsure of using it - i will learn for the next few vids

author: @MongrelShark
text: Great news mate. How long can you keep this space? I'm guessing your going to pay the rent with graphene sales? I really need to get a bit more organized and do something like this. Thanks for the inspiration.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's the plan - it will mean a trade off between total open source and trade secret but we live in a realistic world and sales will cover the sharing and experimentation of other things.

replies.author: @MongrelShark
replies.text:
I have a feeling even if you open source fully, people will still pay, plus donate for no product. I want some graphene for eg, but cant be bothered making it as already too busy with electronics and lousy at chemistry. (unfortunately too broke atm). Happy to pay you to sell me some of your open source formula. A lot less likely to buy a secret formula though. I reckon you could do it better, cheaper and faster than I could. Due to your experience, bulk supply, and quality lab gear.  Plus you wont mess up my kitchen doing it.
You can still protect your formula from unauthorized commercial manufacture (in theroy at least, lawyers are expensive) with some kind of open source/ creative commons licene. Keep it free to reproduce for home and non profit/low profit. But if some chemical giant makes a ton of it and undercuts you, without paying your license fee (which should be structured so its very hard to undercut you and maintain quality) Then you sue them for 80% of the profits. Open source software has already set the scene for that. Look at Ubuntu for eg.

Another example is the pay what you like restaurants. They do really well. Or Charles Eisenstein's books, he made more in donations giving away free digital copies, than he did through the publishers. As some people pay a lot more than RRP. It makes up for people that dont pay, then there are those like me that read and enjoyed a free copy, couldn't pay, so shared like crazy and got others to spread it via word of mouth.

The patent system was supposed to allow that sort of thing, but as we have discussed before, its got many failings now due to corporate corruption watering it down to a game of highly paid law firms.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Mongrel Shark
do you know - I never thought about creative commons but i like your suggestion - I am uncomfortable with secrets but aware of all the sharks - no pun intended - but this suggestion looks like the way to go here - thanks mate

replies.author: @MongrelShark
replies.text: No worries. Its the way I want to go with some of my electronic ideas. Which is part of the reason I use a YouTube license, protecting my intellectual property... Problem is there dont seem to be any precedents of it protecting a tech development.... If you do get a good license drafted up. let me know :) I'll do the same for you. As I said, good lawyers are expensive.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate will do

author: @leonardjanus5538
text:
I could contribute, sharing my tools and knowledge.
I like "hackerspaces" and "brain stormings".
For example I have some large format piezo plotters, from my old job.
They was modified to print also mild-solvent inks, and I made inks myself .
I have chemicals from that old job, like glycols, emulsifiers, surfactants, resins and so on.
I'm a "men in shed".
My world is "all around electric energy".
If you want, please contact me by my e-mail.

author: @IONICSGLASS
text: Greetings,, I have been watching your graphene videos and have been inspired to start graphene research and development myself! I am a scientist, photonics has been my main passion,, I have a small company here in Phoenix, Arizona USA and have a fairly well equiped lab with 20+ lasers, intense pulsed light engines and vacuum deposition equip.. and..... I was thinking of starting a laser ablation / deposition "in vacuo" to coat thin films of carbon... I keep scouring for more information however there is so little info out there as of now...  Anyway,  thank you for sharing your knowledge and making the videos,, I have really enjoyed!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Hiya David - thanks for that - you are right there is very little info out there - which is one of my main motivations as i believe we progress faster if ideas are shared so I am glad you liked them

author: @directeeccks
text: This is great.  The audio sounds pretty good in there too.  It also seems you may be on to a like a "maker/hacker space" with that invitation to come down.  I don't have any of those where I live.   New York and California has a ton of them scattered all across their cities.  Who knows you may get someone down there with experience with cnc/3dprinting or things of that sort to contribute.  Printed graphene  circuits comes to mind. Let alone any other materials you may create in your new workspace.  I mean all printers are is a distribution of materials in a controlled manner and you are very knowledgeable on materials.  Congrats!!!! This made my day.
